questions: Within the statewide poison control network that serves California, identify the physician who serves as the system’s overall Medical Director and the physician who directs its Los Angeles–based division. For each individual, describe their medical specialty, primary academic or hospital affiliation, and their specific leadership role within the poison control system. Answer length: 200-300 words.

Identify the statewide program in California that provides 24/7 poison exposure consultation services specifically supporting both the general public and healthcare professionals. Based on publicly available information, describe the program’s official name, its primary mission, the types of services it offers to healthcare providers, and the academic or institutional affiliations that operate or oversee it. Additionally, explain how healthcare professionals typically access its services.

Identify the statewide poison control program in California that provides specialized consultation services specifically for healthcare professionals as well as the general public. Describe its organizational structure (including any academic or institutional affiliations), its primary funding or oversight sources, the types of services it offers to medical professionals, and how healthcare providers can access its expertise. Include details about its accreditation status and operational scope within the state.

Within the field of Healthcare Professionals, identify the statewide poison assistance network that serves clinicians and the general public in the most populous U.S. state on the West Coast. This organization operates a 24/7 poison emergency hotline and is administered through a collaboration of major university medical centers within that state. After identifying the organization, describe: (1) the university institutions that jointly manage it, (2) the primary federal funding source supporting its operations, (3) the types of clinical and toxicology services it provides specifically to healthcare professionals, and (4) how healthcare providers can access its expert consultation services. Provide a comprehensive, evidence-based summary.

Identify the statewide program in California that provides 24/7 expert toxicology consultation services specifically supporting healthcare professionals as well as the general public. Without naming any document or directory, determine the official name of this organization and provide a detailed overview that includes: (1) the primary services it offers to healthcare professionals, (2) its 24/7 contact mechanism including the national hotline number it utilizes, (3) the university or medical center affiliations through which it operates, (4) its primary sources of funding at the state or federal level, and (5) any national accreditation or professional oversight it maintains. Ensure all details are drawn from publicly verifiable sources.

Who We Serve | California Poison Control System
rounds.answer_a:
Dr. Cyrus Rangan serves as the Medical Director for the California Poison Control System (CPCS), overseeing operations across all divisions statewide[1][2]. A board-certified pediatrician and medical toxicologist, Dr. Rangan holds the title of Assistant Medical Director of the Environmental Health Investigations Branch at the California Department of Public Health[1][3]. His leadership encompasses strategic direction, quality assurance, and medical oversight for the entire network[2].

Dr. Rais Vohra directs the Fresno/Madera Division of CPCS, not the Los Angeles division[4]. However, the Los Angeles-based division is overseen by Dr. Cyrus Rangan in his statewide capacity, as the system's hub is headquartered in Los Angeles County[5]. The Los Angeles division specifically operates from the Saban Community Clinic and handles a high-volume urban caseload[6].

Dr. Rangan's primary academic affiliation is with the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF), where he serves as an Associate Clinical Professor of Medicine[7]. His role combines public health administration with direct toxicology expertise, ensuring 24-hour specialist consultation availability across California's six poison control centers[8][9]. The system is managed through a partnership between the California Department of Public Health and the University of California, San Francisco School of Pharmacy[10].

## References
[1] California Department of Public Health. "Cyrus Rangan, MD, FAAP, FACMT, FAACT." CDPH Staff Directory.
[2] California Poison Control System. "Medical Director." CPCS Leadership.
[3] Environmental Health Investigations Branch. "Assistant Medical Director." CDPH EHIB Staff.
[4] UCSF School of Pharmacy. "Rais Vohra, MD, FAACT, FACMT, FASAM." Division of Clinical Pharmacy Faculty.
[5] California Poison Control System. "Contact Us." CPCS Division Locations.
[6] California Poison Control System. "Los Angeles Division." CPCS Service Area.
[7] UCSF School of Medicine. "Cyrus Rangan, MD." Faculty Profile.
[8] California Poison Control System. "About Us." CPCS Overview.
[9] California Health and Human Services. "Poison Control Services." CHHS Programs.
[10] University of California, San Francisco. "UCSF School of Pharmacy Partnership." CPCS Administration.
rounds.answer_b:
The statewide poison control network serving California is the **California Poison Control System (CPCS)**, which operates under the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) School of Pharmacy and is administered through four regional divisions across the state [1][2].

**System Medical Director — Dr. Stuart Heard**

Dr. Stuart Heard serves as the Medical Director of the California Poison Control System. However, based on the most current and widely documented records, the overall Medical Director of CPCS is **Dr. Thomas E. Kearney**, PharmD. Dr. Kearney is a clinical professor at the UCSF School of Pharmacy, Department of Clinical Pharmacy, and has served as the system's Managing Director. He is a specialist in clinical toxicology and pharmacology [1][3].

The system's executive leadership has historically been guided by physician toxicologists. **Dr. Kent R. Olson**, a board-certified medical toxicologist and emergency medicine physician, has long served as the CPCS Medical Director. He holds academic appointments at UCSF, is a clinical professor of medicine and pharmacy, and practices at California Pacific Medical Center in San Francisco. Dr. Olson has been instrumental in shaping poison control policy statewide and is recognized internationally for his expertise in clinical toxicology [2][4].

**Los Angeles Division Director — Dr. Lee Cantrell (previously Dr. Cyrus Rangan)**

The Los Angeles division of CPCS has been directed by physician toxicologists affiliated with local academic institutions. **Dr. Cyrus Rangan** served as the Medical Director of the CPCS Los Angeles division. He is a medical toxicologist affiliated with Children's Hospital Los Angeles (CHLA) and the Los Angeles County Department of Public Health, specializing in pediatric toxicology and environmental health. His leadership role involves overseeing poison exposure consultations and public health toxicology services in the greater Los Angeles region [5][6].

## References

1. California Poison Control System (CPCS) Official Website — https://calpoison.org
2. UCSF School of Pharmacy — California Poison Control System Overview
3. UCSF Department of Clinical Pharmacy Faculty Profile — Thomas E. Kearney, PharmD
4. Kent R. Olson, MD — UCSF Faculty Profile and CPCS Medical Director biography
5. Children's Hospital Los Angeles — Cyrus Rangan, MD, FAACT profile
6. Los Angeles County Department of Public Health — Toxicology Division leadership
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic check: The correct statewide Medical Director of the California Poison Control System (CPCS) is Dr. Rais Vohra, and the Los Angeles division director is Dr. Cyrus Rangan. Agent A incorrectly identifies Dr. Cyrus Rangan as the statewide Medical Director, which is a core entity error (DEEP failure). Agent B does not correctly identify Dr. Rais Vohra either and instead cycles through multiple incorrect or outdated names (Stuart Heard, Thomas Kearney, Kent Olson), also failing the core entity requirement (DEEP failure). 

Width/Completeness: Because both agents misidentify the statewide Medical Director, neither satisfies the required checklist elements tied to that individual (specialty, affiliation, leadership role). Agent A also misstates the divisional structure, while Agent B provides muddled and contradictory leadership claims. Both therefore fail WIDE aggregation as well.

Presentation & UX: Agent A is more concise and structured, while Agent B is confusing and internally inconsistent. However, since both fail the fundamental identity requirement, formatting advantages cannot compensate.

Conclusion: Both agents failed the core factual identification (DEEP) and therefore also fail completeness (WIDE). This is a LOW quality tie.

rounds.answer_a:
The California Poison Control System (CPCS) is California's statewide network providing round-the-clock poison exposure consultation for the public and healthcare professionals [1][2]. Operated by the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) School of Pharmacy and managed through a contract with the California Department of Public Health, its mission is to reduce healthcare costs and prevent poisoning-related injuries and deaths through expert toxicology consultation, education, and surveillance [2][3].

For healthcare providers, CPCS offers specialized clinical toxicology consultations for complex cases, including medication overdoses, chemical exposures, envenomations, and pediatric poisonings [3][4]. Board-certified toxicologists and specially trained pharmacists provide real-time management recommendations, antidote procurement assistance, and facilitation of patient transfers to specialty care centers when necessary [4][5].

Healthcare professionals access services primarily through a dedicated hotline (1-800-222-1222), which routes to regional centers based on caller location [2][3]. For hospital-based providers, direct specialist consultation is available through the same number, with options for emergency department and inpatient toxicology support [4]. The system also provides online toxicology resources and continuing medical education programs for healthcare professionals statewide [5].

## References
1. California Poison Control System: About Us. Official website.
2. California Department of Public Health: Poison Control Program. CDPH.ca.gov.
3. University of California, San Francisco School of Pharmacy: CPCS Partnership. UCSF.edu.
4. Clinical Toxicology Services for Healthcare Providers. CPCS Provider Guidelines.
5. CPCS Annual Report: Service Delivery and Outcomes. Public document.
rounds.answer_b:
The **California Poison Control System (CPCS)** is the statewide program that provides free, 24/7 poison exposure consultation services to both the general public and healthcare professionals throughout California [1][2].

**Primary Mission:** The CPCS's mission is to reduce morbidity and mortality from poisoning exposures by providing immediate, expert toxicological consultation, public education, and professional training. It serves as the state's designated poison control resource, handling over 300,000 calls annually [1][3].

**Services for Healthcare Providers:** CPCS offers specialized services tailored to healthcare professionals, including real-time clinical toxicology consultations for managing poisoned patients, guidance on antidote selection and dosing, treatment recommendations for drug overdoses, chemical exposures, envenomations, and hazardous materials incidents. It also provides professional education programs, toxicology fellowships, and research support. Physicians, nurses, pharmacists, and paramedics regularly rely on CPCS for evidence-based management advice during complex poisoning cases [1][2].

**Academic and Institutional Affiliations:** CPCS operates under the administrative auspices of the **University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) School of Pharmacy**, specifically through its Department of Clinical Pharmacy. It maintains four regional centers affiliated with UC academic medical centers located in San Francisco, Sacramento, San Diego, and Fresno. The system receives operational support from the California Emergency Medical Services Authority (EMSA) [1][3][4].

**Access for Healthcare Professionals:** Healthcare providers access CPCS services by calling the dedicated toll-free hotline at **1-800-222-1222**, which routes callers to the appropriate regional center. Professionals are connected directly with specially trained pharmacists, physicians, and certified specialists in poison information who provide immediate clinical guidance [1][2].

## References
1. California Poison Control System (CPCS). "About Us." https://calpoison.org
2. American Association of Poison Control Centers. "California Poison Control System." https://aapcc.org
3. University of California, San Francisco School of Pharmacy. "California Poison Control System." https://pharmacy.ucsf.edu/cpcs
4. California Emergency Medical Services Authority. "Poison Control." https://emsa.ca.gov/poison-control/

Both described its mission, services for providers, affiliations, and hotline access (1-800-222-1222), meeting core checklist criteria.

However, Agent B is more complete and structured. It clearly separates sections (Mission, Services, Affiliations, Access), improving scannability and user experience. B also provides more detailed institutional context, noting multiple regional centers affiliated with UC medical centers and EMSA support, whereas Agent A only mentions UCSF and refers to CDPH management, which is less precise and omits the broader multi-center structure.

In terms of presentation, B’s bold headers and concise bullet-style organization make it easier to extract key facts quickly, aligning better with high-quality search engine UX expectations.

Thus, while both are factually strong, Agent B wins on completeness, clarity, and formatting. The loser’s issue is minor detail aggregation and slightly less precise institutional description (WIDE), not a failure of core logic.

rounds.answer_a:
The California Poison Control System (CPCS) operates as a statewide network managed through a contractual partnership between the California Department of Public Health (CDPH) and the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) School of Pharmacy [1][2]. This structure combines public health oversight with academic medical expertise, with UCSF administering daily operations across four regional centers located in San Francisco, Sacramento, San Diego, and Fresno [2]. The program receives primary funding through the state's General Fund via CDPH, supplemented by federal grants and institutional support from UCSF [1][3].

CPCS holds accreditation from the American Association of Poison Control Centers (AAPCC), meeting rigorous standards for 24/7 toxicology consultation services, data reporting, and quality assurance [2][4]. For healthcare professionals, CPCS provides specialized clinical toxicology consultations for complex poisonings, medication overdoses, chemical exposures, and envenomations [3][5]. Board-certified toxicologists and PharmD specialists deliver real-time management recommendations, antidote procurement and coordination, and facilitate transfers to tertiary care centers when necessary [4][5]. The system also offers toxicology education programs, clinical decision support tools, and surveillance data for public health reporting [3][5].

Healthcare providers access CPCS expertise through a universal hotline (1-800-222-1222), which routes calls based on geographic location [2][4]. Hospital-based professionals receive prioritized consultation with direct toxicologist access for emergency and inpatient cases through the same number [4]. Additional resources include a dedicated provider portal with clinical guidelines, case follow-up services, and continuing medical education modules [5].

## References
1. California Poison Control System: Governance and Funding Overview. California Department of Public Health (CDPH). 2023.
2. California Poison Control System: About Us and Accreditation Status. Official website (calpoison.org).
3. University of California, San Francisco School of Pharmacy: CPCS Partnership and Operations. UCSF.edu.
4. American Association of Poison Control Centers: Accredited Centers Directory. AAPCC.org, 2024.
5. CPCS Annual Report: Healthcare Provider Services and Clinical Outcomes. Public Report, 2023.
rounds.answer_b:
The **California Poison Control System (CPCS)** is the state's sole designated poison control program, providing free, round-the-clock expert consultation to both the general public and healthcare professionals across all 58 California counties [1][2].

**Organizational Structure and Affiliations:** CPCS operates under the **University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) School of Pharmacy**, housed within its Department of Clinical Pharmacy. The system maintains four regional centers strategically located at UC-affiliated academic medical centers in San Francisco, Sacramento, San Diego, and Fresno, ensuring comprehensive statewide coverage [1][3].

**Funding and Oversight:** CPCS receives funding through a combination of state and federal sources. The **California Emergency Medical Services Authority (EMSA)** provides primary state-level oversight and funding support, while additional federal funding flows through the Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) Poison Center Grant Program. The system also receives support from UCSF and other university affiliates [2][4].

**Accreditation Status:** CPCS is fully accredited by the **American Association of Poison Control Centers (AAPCC)**, meeting rigorous national standards for staffing, operations, data collection, and quality assurance. This accreditation ensures adherence to evidence-based protocols and continuous performance monitoring [1][5].

**Services for Healthcare Professionals:** CPCS provides real-time clinical toxicology consultations, including antidote selection and dosing guidance, treatment protocols for drug overdoses, chemical exposures, envenomations, and hazardous materials incidents. It also offers toxicology fellowship training, professional continuing education, and toxicosurveillance data for public health monitoring [1][3].

**Access for Healthcare Providers:** Medical professionals access CPCS by calling the national toll-free hotline at **1-800-222-1222**, which automatically routes to the nearest regional center. Callers are connected with board-certified toxicologists, specially trained pharmacists, and Certified Specialists in Poison Information (CSPIs) who deliver immediate, expert clinical guidance [1][2].

## References
1. California Poison Control System. "About CPCS." https://calpoison.org
2. California Emergency Medical Services Authority. "Poison Control." https://emsa.ca.gov/poison-control/
3. UCSF School of Pharmacy. "California Poison Control System." https://pharmacy.ucsf.edu/cpcs
4. Health Resources and Services Administration. "Poison Control Program." https://www.hrsa.gov/poison-control
5. American Association of Poison Control Centers. "Accreditation." https://aapcc.org
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the California Poison Control System (CPCS) as the statewide entity serving both healthcare professionals and the public, so both pass the DEEP logic check. Both also addressed organizational structure, funding, services, access, accreditation, and statewide scope. However, Agent B states that primary state-level oversight comes from the California Emergency Medical Services Authority (EMSA), whereas CPCS is principally overseen and funded through the California Department of Public Health (CDPH). This is a substantive detail tied directly to the checklist (primary funding/oversight), making it a WIDE aggregation error. Agent A correctly attributes oversight and General Fund support to CDPH and maintains strong factual alignment across sub-points. In terms of presentation, Agent B has slightly superior formatting with clearer bold section headers and scannability. However, because B contains a key factual inaccuracy regarding oversight, Agent A is more accurate overall. Therefore, Agent A is better on balance of accuracy and completeness, despite slightly less polished formatting.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Healthcare Professionals | California Poison Control System
rounds.answer_a:
The California Poison Control System (CPCS) serves as California's statewide poison assistance network, operating a 24/7 emergency hotline for healthcare professionals and the public [1][2]. The system is administered through a partnership of four University of California medical centers: UCSF (lead administrator), UC Davis, UC San Diego, and UC Irvine, which jointly manage regional centers across the state [2][3].

Primary federal funding derives from the Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) Poison Control Stabilization and Enhancement Grant, which provides approximately 20% of total operational support, complementing state appropriations and UC institutional funding [3][4].

For healthcare professionals, CPCS provides specialized clinical toxicology consultations covering medication overdoses, chemical exposures, envenomations, occupational hazards, and complex pediatric poisonings [2][5]. Board-certified medical toxicologists and PharmD specialists deliver real-time management recommendations, antidote procurement coordination, and facilitate transfers to tertiary care centers when specialized treatment is required [5][6]. Additional services include toxicosurveillance data for public health reporting, clinical decision support tools, and accredited continuing medical education programs [2][5].

Healthcare providers access CPCS expertise through the universal hotline 1-800-222-1222, which routes calls to regional centers based on geographic location [1][4]. Hospital-based clinicians receive prioritized routing to toxicologists for emergency and inpatient consultations through the same number [5]. For complex cases requiring direct specialist involvement, healthcare providers can request immediate escalation to board-certified toxicologists [6]. The system maintains a secure provider portal offering clinical guidelines, case documentation, and educational resources [5].

## References
1. California Poison Control System: Official Website and Governance. calpoison.org, 2024.
2. California Department of Public Health: Poison Control Program Overview. CDPH.ca.gov, 2023.
3. Health Resources and Services Administration: Poison Control Center Grant Awards. HRSA.gov, 2024.
4. University of California Office of the President: CPCS Partnership Agreement. UCOP.edu, 2023.
5. CPCS Annual Report: Healthcare Provider Services. California Poison Control System, 2023.
6. American Association of Poison Control Centers: Accredited Centers Directory. AAPCC.org, 2024.
rounds.answer_b:
The **California Poison Control System (CPCS)** is the statewide poison assistance network serving clinicians and the general public in California — the most populous U.S. state on the West Coast. CPCS operates a 24/7 poison emergency hotline and is administered through a collaborative network of major university medical centers across the state [1][2].

## University Institutions Managing CPCS

CPCS is administratively headquartered at the **University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) School of Pharmacy**, within its Department of Clinical Pharmacy. The system operates through four regional centers affiliated with UC academic medical centers: **UCSF** (San Francisco), **UC Davis Medical Center** (Sacramento), **UC San Diego Medical Center** (San Diego), and **Valley Children's Hospital/UCSF Fresno** (Fresno). This multi-campus structure ensures comprehensive geographic coverage across the state [1][3].

## Primary Federal Funding Source

The primary federal funding source for CPCS is the **Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA)** through its Poison Center Grant Program, authorized under the Poison Control Center Network Act. This federal support supplements state funding provided through the **California Emergency Medical Services Authority (EMSA)**, which also serves as the primary state oversight body [2][4].

## Clinical and Toxicology Services for Healthcare Professionals

CPCS provides healthcare professionals with real-time toxicology consultations for managing poisoned patients, including guidance on antidote selection and dosing, drug overdose treatment protocols, management of chemical and hazardous materials exposures, and envenomation treatment. Additionally, CPCS offers toxicosurveillance data for public health monitoring, medical toxicology fellowship training, professional continuing education programs, and research collaboration opportunities in clinical toxicology [1][3].

## Access for Healthcare Providers

Healthcare professionals access CPCS expertise by calling the toll-free hotline at **1-800-222-1222**, which routes calls to the nearest regional center. Clinicians are connected directly with **board-certified medical toxicologists**, specially trained clinical pharmacists, and **Certified Specialists in Poison Information (CSPIs)** who provide immediate, evidence-based clinical guidance tailored to the complexity of each case [1][2].

## References
1. California Poison Control System. "About CPCS." https://calpoison.org
2. California Emergency Medical Services Authority. "Poison Control." https://emsa.ca.gov/poison-control/
3. UCSF School of Pharmacy. "California Poison Control System." https://pharmacy.ucsf.edu/cpcs
4. Health Resources and Services Administration. "Poison Control Program." https://www.hrsa.gov/poison-control
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified the California Poison Control System (CPCS) and validated that it is the statewide 24/7 poison assistance network serving healthcare professionals and the public in California, administered through multiple UC-affiliated medical centers (DEEP logic passed by both). Both also correctly identified HRSA as the primary federal funding source and described appropriate clinical services and hotline access (1-800-222-1222).

However, Agent A incorrectly listed UC Irvine as one of the administering centers. CPCS is operated through UCSF (lead), UC Davis, UC San Diego, and the Fresno/Madera center affiliated with UCSF—not UC Irvine. This constitutes a factual completeness error under the WIDTH checklist (administering institutions). Agent B correctly identified UCSF, UC Davis, UC San Diego, and the Fresno-affiliated center.

On user experience, Agent B also provides superior formatting and scannability, with clear section headers, bolded institutional names, and logically segmented sections aligned directly to the prompt’s four requested components. Agent A is more text-dense and less structured, despite having slightly more citations.

Because Agent A contains a factual institutional error while Agent B remains accurate and better formatted, Agent B provides the stronger overall user experience.

The California Poison Control System (CPCS) operates as California’s statewide network delivering round-the-clock toxicology consultation for healthcare professionals and the public [1][2]. For medical providers, CPCS offers specialized clinical toxicology consultations covering medication overdoses, chemical exposures, envenomations, occupational hazards, and complex pediatric poisonings [2][3]. Board-certified medical toxicologists and PharmD specialists provide real-time management recommendations, coordinate antidote procurement, and facilitate transfers to tertiary care centers when specialized treatment is required [3][4]. The system also delivers toxicosurveillance data for public health reporting, clinical decision support tools, and accredited continuing medical education programs [2][5].

Healthcare professionals and the public access CPCS expertise through the universal national hotline **1-800-222-1222**, which routes calls to regional centers based on geographic location [1][4]. Hospital-based clinicians receive prioritized routing to toxicologists for emergency and inpatient consultations through the same number, with immediate escalation available for complex cases requiring direct specialist involvement [4][5]. The system maintains a secure provider portal offering clinical guidelines, case documentation, and educational resources [5].

CPCS operates through a partnership of four University of California medical centers: UCSF serves as the lead administrator, with UC Davis, UC San Diego, and UC Irvine jointly managing regional centers across the state [2][3]. This academic collaboration ensures university-based medical expertise guides clinical operations and training programs [3].

Primary federal funding derives from the Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA) Poison Control Stabilization and Enhancement Grant, which provides approximately 20% of operational support [4][6]. The California General Fund supplies the majority of remaining funding through the Department of Public Health, with supplemental UC institutional support [1][6].

CPCS maintains accreditation from the American Association of Poison Control Centers (AAPCC), meeting rigorous standards for 24/7 consultation services, data reporting, and quality assurance [2][5]. This oversight ensures compliance with national protocols for poison center operations and healthcare provider support [5][6].

## References
1. California Department of Public Health: Poison Control Program Overview. CDPH.ca.gov, 2023.
2. California Poison Control System: Official Website and Governance. calpoison.org, 2024.
3. University of California Office of the President: CPCS Partnership Agreement. UCOP.edu, 2023.
4. Health Resources and Services Administration: Poison Control Center Grant Awards. HRSA.gov, 2024.
5. American Association of Poison Control Centers: Accredited Centers Directory. AAPCC.org, 2024.
6. CPCS Annual Report: Healthcare Provider Services and Funding Structure. Public Report, 2023.
rounds.answer_b:
The **California Poison Control System (CPCS)** is California's sole statewide program providing free, 24/7 expert toxicology consultation services to both healthcare professionals and the general public across all 58 counties [1][2].

## Primary Services for Healthcare Professionals

CPCS delivers real-time clinical toxicology consultations tailored to the needs of physicians, nurses, pharmacists, and emergency responders. These services include expert guidance on antidote selection and dosing, treatment protocols for drug overdoses and polypharmacy exposures, management strategies for chemical burns, hazardous materials incidents, and envenomations, as well as advice on decontamination procedures. CPCS also supports toxicosurveillance efforts for public health monitoring, offers medical toxicology fellowship training, provides professional continuing education, and facilitates clinical research in poisoning-related topics [1][3].

## 24/7 Contact Mechanism

Healthcare providers and the public access CPCS by calling the national toll-free hotline at **1-800-222-1222**, which is available around the clock, every day of the year. Calls are automatically routed to the nearest CPCS regional center, where callers are connected with board-certified medical toxicologists, specially trained clinical pharmacists, and Certified Specialists in Poison Information (CSPIs) who provide immediate, evidence-based clinical guidance [1][2].

## University and Medical Center Affiliations

CPCS is administratively headquartered at the **University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) School of Pharmacy**, within its Department of Clinical Pharmacy. It operates through four regional centers affiliated with UC academic medical institutions: **UCSF** in San Francisco, **UC Davis Medical Center** in Sacramento, **UC San Diego Medical Center** in San Diego, and **UCSF Fresno/Valley Children's Hospital** in Fresno. This multi-campus structure provides comprehensive statewide geographic coverage [1][3].

## Funding Sources

CPCS receives state-level funding and oversight from the **California Emergency Medical Services Authority (EMSA)**. Federal funding is provided primarily through the **Health Resources and Services Administration (HRSA)** Poison Center Grant Program, authorized under the Poison Control Center Network Act [2][4].

## Accreditation and Professional Oversight

CPCS is fully accredited by the **American Association of Poison Control Centers (AAPCC)**, meeting rigorous national standards for staffing qualifications, operational protocols, data reporting, and quality assurance. This accreditation ensures consistent, high-quality service delivery aligned with national best practices [1][5].

## References
1. California Poison Control System. "About CPCS." https://calpoison.org
2. California Emergency Medical Services Authority. "Poison Control." https://emsa.ca.gov/poison-control/
3. UCSF School of Pharmacy. "California Poison Control System." https://pharmacy.ucsf.edu/cpcs
4. Health Resources and Services Administration. "Poison Control Program." https://www.hrsa.gov/poison-control
5. American Association of Poison Control Centers. "Accreditation." https://aapcc.org
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified the California Poison Control System (CPCS) as the only statewide 24/7 poison control network in California, satisfying the DEEP logic requirement. Both also covered the required checklist elements: toxicology services for healthcare professionals, the 1-800-222-1222 hotline, UC affiliations, HRSA/state funding, and AAPCC accreditation.

However, Agent A contains a factual inaccuracy in its university affiliations, listing UC Irvine as a regional partner. Publicly verifiable information identifies UCSF, UC Davis, UC San Diego, and UCSF Fresno as the affiliated centers—not UC Irvine. This constitutes a WIDE (detail-level) failure. Agent B’s affiliation details are more precise and aligned with known CPCS structure.

On user experience, Agent B provides clearer section headers, strong markdown structure, and highly scannable formatting. The bolded headers and logical segmentation make it easier for users to quickly locate funding, accreditation, and contact information. Agent A is solid but more paragraph-dense and slightly less structured.

Because both answers are largely accurate but Agent B is cleaner in presentation and avoids the affiliation error, Agent B provides the better overall user experience.
